incorrects.description: Student selected 5 — only counted Red, not both categories

steps.text: You found Red's value. The question asks for Yellow OR Red together. Add both bars to find how many in all.

incorrects.description: Student selected 6 — only counted Yellow, not both categories

steps.text: You found Yellow's value. The question asks for Yellow OR Red together. Add both bars to find how many in all.

incorrects.description: Student selected 14 — added Yellow, Red, and Green instead of just Yellow and Red

steps.text: You added too many categories. Only add Yellow and Red. Green comes in the next step when we compare.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Yellow bar (6) and Red bar (5) pulse in sequence, then total 11 appears

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Yellow has 6 students. Red has 5 students. When we add them together, 6 plus 5 equals 11. That's how many chose Yellow or Red in all.

incorrects.description: Student selected 4 — gave Green's value instead of the difference

steps.text: You found Green's value. The question asks how many MORE 11 is than 4. Subtract to find the difference.

incorrects.description: Student selected 11 — gave the total from step 1 instead of the difference

steps.text: That's the total from step 1. Now compare it to Green's 4. How many more is 11 than 4? Subtract to find the difference.

incorrects.description: Student selected 15 — added 11 and 4 instead of subtracting

steps.text: The question asks how many MORE, not how many in all. When you compare two amounts, subtract the smaller from the larger.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Green bar (4) extends to match against the total 11, showing difference of 7

steps.text: This is tricky, so let me show you. We have 11 from step 1. Green has 4. To find how many more 11 is than 4, we subtract: 11 minus 4 equals 7. That's the difference.
